358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
365	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
368	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
369	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
370	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
371	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
372	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
373	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
378	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
379	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
380	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
381	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
382	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
383	  equivalence.
384	\end{proof}
385	
386	\begin{lemma}[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
387	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
389	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
390	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
391	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
392	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
393	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
394	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
395	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
400	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
401	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
402	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
406	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
408	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
409	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
410	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
411	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
412	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	\begin{proof}
416	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
417	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
418	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
419	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
423	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
425	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
426	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
427	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
428	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
429	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
430	  parallel pair
431	  \[
432	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
433	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
434	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
435	  \]
436	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
437	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
438	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
439	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
444	  Over each open \(U\) the module
445	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the very
446	  definition of the relative tensor product, the quotient of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
447	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) by the subgroup generated by
448	  the elements \((r \cdot p) \otimes q - p \otimes (r \cdot q)\); equivalently it is
449	  the coequalizer of the two \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear action maps displayed above. Since
450	  colimits of abelian-group presheaves are computed objectwise, these objectwise
451	  coequalizers assemble into a coequalizer in
452	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\); naturality in \(U\) is the
453	  compatibility of the action maps with the restriction maps of
454	  \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\).
455	\end{proof}
456	
457	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
458	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
460	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
461	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
462	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
463	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
464	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
465	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
466	  the sheafification adjunction
467	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
468	  presheaves of modules, and write
469	  \[
470	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
471	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
472	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
473	  \]
474	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
475	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
476	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
477	  \[
478	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
479	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
480	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
481	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
482	  \]
483	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
484	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
485	\end{lemma}
486	
487	\begin{proof}
488	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
489	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
490	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
491	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
492	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
493	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
494	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
495	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
496	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
497	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
498	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
499	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
500	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
501	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
502	
503	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
504	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
505	  is the objectwise relative tensor
506	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
507	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
508	  maps
509	  \[
510	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
511	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
512	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
513	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
514	  \]
515	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
516	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
517	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
518	  pair
519	  \[
520	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
521	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
522	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
523	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
524	  \]
525	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
526	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
527	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
528	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
529	
530	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
531	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
532	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
533	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
534	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
535	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
536	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
537	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
538	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
539	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
540	  comparison maps
541	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
542	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
543	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
544	  isomorphisms.
545	
546	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
547	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
548	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
549	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
550	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
551	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
555	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
557	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
558	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
559	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
560	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
561	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
562	  \[
563	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
564	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
565	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
566	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
567	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
568	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
569	  \]
570	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
571	  arguments.
572	\end{corollary}
573	
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
576	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
577	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
578	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
579	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
580	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
581	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
582	  \[
583	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
584	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
585	    \qquad
586	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
587	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
588	  \]
589	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
590	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
591	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
592	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
593	  \[
594	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
595	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
596	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
597	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
598	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
599	  \]
600	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
601	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
602	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
603	  morphism
604	  \[
605	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
606	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
607	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
608	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
609	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
610	  \]
611	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
612	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
613	  \[
614	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
615	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
616	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
617	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
618	  \]
619	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
620	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
621	  composite
622	  \[
623	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
624	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
625	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
626	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
627	  \]
628	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
629	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
630	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
634	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
636	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
637	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
638	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
639	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
641	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
642	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
643	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
644	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
645	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
646	  %  (formulation omitted)."
647	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
648	  power isomorphisms).}
649	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
650	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
651	  \[
652	    \mu_{m,m'} :
653	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
654	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
655	  \]
656	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
657	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
658	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
659	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
660	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
661	\end{lemma}
662	
663	\begin{proof}
664	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
665	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
666	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
667	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
668	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
669	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
670	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
671	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
672	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
673	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
674	
675	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
676	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
677	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
678	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
679	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
680	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
681	  sheaf tensor products
682	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
683	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
684	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
685	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
686	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
687	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
688	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
689	  of the tensor product is never used.
690	
691	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
692	  \(m = 0\),
693	  \[
694	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
695	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
696	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
697	  \]
698	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
699	  recursive definition
700	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
701	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
702	  \[
703	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
704	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
705	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
706	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
707	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
708	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
709	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
710	  \]
711	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
712	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
713	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
714	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
715	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
716	  \[
717	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
718	      \mathcal{L}
719	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
720	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
721	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
722	  \]
723	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
724	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
725	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
726	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
727	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
728	
729	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
730	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
731	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
732	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
733	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
734	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
735	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
736	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
737	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
738	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
739	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
740	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
741	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
742	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
743	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
744	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
745	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
746	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
747	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
748	\end{proof}
749	
750	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
751	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
752	
753	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
754	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
755	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
756	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
757	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
758	global sections.
759	
760	\begin{definition}\leanok
761	[Section multiplication]
762	  \label{def:sectionMul}
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
764	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
765	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
766	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
767	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
768	  \[
769	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
770	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
771	    \;\longrightarrow\;
772	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
773	  \]
774	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
775	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
776	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
777	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
778	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
779	  the sheafification unit
780	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
781	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
782	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
783	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
784	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
785	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
786	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
787	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
788	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
789	\end{definition}
790	
791	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
792	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
794	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
795	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
796	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
797	  the two composites
798	  \[
799	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
800	    \longrightarrow
801	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
802	    \cong
803	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
804	  \]
805	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
806	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
807	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
808	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
809	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
810	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
811	\end{lemma}
812	
813	\begin{proof}
814	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
815	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
816	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
817	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
818	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
819	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
820	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
821	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
822	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
823	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
824	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
825	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
826	  section multiplications.
827	\end{proof}
828	
829	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
830	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
831	
832	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
833	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
834	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
835	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
836	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
837	
838	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
839	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
840	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
841	  \mathlibok
842	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
843	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
844	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
845	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
846	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
847	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
848	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
849	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
850	  \(1 \in A_0\).
851	\end{lemma}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
854	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
855	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
856	  \mathlibok
857	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
858	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
859	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
860	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
861	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
862	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
863	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
864	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
865	  extension of the graded action.
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
869	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
871	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
872	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
873	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
874	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
875	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
876	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
878	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
879	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
880	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
881	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
882	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
883	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
884	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
885	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
886	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
887	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
888	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
889	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
890	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
891	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
892	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
893	  multiplication is the composite
894	  \[
895	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
896	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
897	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
898	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
899	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
900	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
901	  \]
902	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
903	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
904	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
905	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
906	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
907	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
908	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
909	\end{lemma}
910	
911	\begin{proof}
912	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
913	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
914	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
915	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
916	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
917	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
918	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
919	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
920	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
921	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
922	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
923	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
924	  commutative-semiring structure.
925	\end{proof}
926	
927	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
928	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
929	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
930	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
931	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
932	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
933	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
934	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
935	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
936	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
937	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
938	  %  $$
939	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
940	  %  =
941	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
942	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
943	  %  $$
944	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
945	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
946	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
947	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
948	  \(X_s\). The family
949	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
950	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
951	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
952	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
953	  action
954	  \[
955	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
956	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
957	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
958	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
959	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
960	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
961	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
962	  \]
963	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
964	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
965	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
966	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
967	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
968	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
969	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
974	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
975	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
976	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
977	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
978	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
979	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
980	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
981	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
982	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
983	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
984	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
985	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
986	\end{proof}
987

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	\begin{definition}
191	
192	\leanok
193	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
194	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
196	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
197	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
198	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
199	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
200	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
201	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
202	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
203	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
204	  charts. Suppose given:
205	  \begin{itemize}
206	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
207	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
208	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
209	      \[
210	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
211	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
212	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
213	      \]
214	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
215	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
216	  \end{itemize}
217	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
218	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
219	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
220	  \((g_{ij})\):
221	  \begin{enumerate}
222	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
223	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
224	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
225	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
226	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
227	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
228	      isomorphisms satisfy
229	      \[
230	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
231	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
232	      \]
233	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
234	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
235	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
236	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
237	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
238	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
239	      condition.
240	  \end{enumerate}
241	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
242	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
244	  construction produces:
245	  \begin{enumerate}
246	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
247	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
248	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
249	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
250	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
251	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
252	  \end{enumerate}
253	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
254	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
255	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
256	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
257	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
258	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
259	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
260	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
261	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
262	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
263	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
264	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
265	
266	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
267	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
268	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
269	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
270	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
271	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
272	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
273	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
274	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
275	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
276	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
277	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
278	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
279	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
280	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
281	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
282	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
283	  built.
284	
285	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
286	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
287	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
288	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
289	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
290	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
291	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
292	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
293	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
294	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
295	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
296	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
297	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
298	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
299	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
300	
301	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
302	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
303	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
304	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
305	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
306	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
307	\end{definition}
308	
309	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
310	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
311	
312	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
313	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
314	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
315	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
316	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
317	
318	\begin{definition}\leanok
319	[Global section of the unit module]
320	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
321	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
322	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
323	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
324	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
325	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
326	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
327	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
328	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
329	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
330	  global function.
331	\end{definition}
332	
333	\begin{definition}\leanok
334	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
335	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
336	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
337	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
338	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
339	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
341	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
342	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
343	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
344	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
345	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
346	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
347	\end{definition}
348	
349	\begin{lemma}\leanok
350	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
351	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
353	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
354	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
355	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
356	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
357	\end{lemma}
358	
359	\begin{proof}
360	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
361	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
362	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
363	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
364	\end{proof}
365	
366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
367	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
368	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
370	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
371	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
372	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
373	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
378	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
379	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\begin{definition}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
386	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
388	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
389	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
390	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
391	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
392	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
393	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
394	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
395	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
396	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
397	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
398	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
399	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
400	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
402	  \[
403	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
404	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
405	  \]
406	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
408	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
409	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
410	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
411	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
412	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
413	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
414	
415	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
416	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
417	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
418	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
419	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
420	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
421	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
422	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
423	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
424	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
425	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
426	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
427	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
428	  free sheaves'' primitive.
429	\end{definition}
430	
431	\begin{lemma}\leanok
432	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
433	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
434	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
435	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
436	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
437	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
438	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
439	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
440	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
441	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
442	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
443	  identity:
444	  \[
445	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
446	  \]
447	\end{lemma}
448	
449	\begin{proof}
450	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
451	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
452	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
453	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
454	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
455	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
456	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
457	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
458	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
459	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
460	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
461	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
462	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
463	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
464	\end{proof}

466	\begin{lemma}\leanok
467	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
468	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
470	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
471	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
472	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
477	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
478	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
479	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
480	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
481	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
482	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
483	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
484	  \[
485	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
486	  \]
487	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
488	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
489	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
490	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
491	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
492	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
493	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
494	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
495	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
496	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
497	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
498	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
499	\end{proof}
500	
501	\begin{definition}
502	
503	\leanok
504	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
505	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
507	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
508	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
509	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
510	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
511	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
512	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
513	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
514	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
515	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
516	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
517	  %  by putting
518	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
519	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
520	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
521	  \[
522	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
523	  \]
524	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
525	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
526	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
527	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
528	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
529	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
530	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
531	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
532	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
533	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
534	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
535	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
536	\end{definition}
537	
538	\begin{definition}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
542	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
544	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
545	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
546	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
547	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
548	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
549	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
550	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
551	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
552	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
553	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
554	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
555	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
556	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
557	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
558	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
559	  \[
560	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
561	  \]
562	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
563	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
564	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
565	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
566	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
567	  \[
568	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
569	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
570	  \]
571	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
572	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
573	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
574	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
575	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
576	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
577	\end{definition}

579	\section{The functor of points}
580	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
581	
582	\begin{definition}
583	
584	\leanok
585	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
586	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
588	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
589	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
590	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
591	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
592	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
593	  % "represents the
594	  %  contravariant functor
595	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
596	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
597	  %  set of all equivalence classes
598	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
599	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
600	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
601	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
602	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
603	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
604	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
605	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
606	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
607	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
608	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
609	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
610	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
611	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
612	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
613	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
614	  \[
615	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
616	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
617	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
618	  \]
619	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
620	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
621	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
622	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
623	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
624	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
625	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
626	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
627	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
628	\end{definition}
629	
630	\section{The universal property}
631	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
632	
633	\begin{theorem}
634	
635	\leanok
636	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
637	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
639	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
640	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
641	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
642	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
643	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
644	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
645	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
646	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
647	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
648	  %  contravariant functor
649	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
650	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
651	  %  set of all equivalence classes
652	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
653	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
654	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
655	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
656	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
657	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
658	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
659	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
660	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
661	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
662	  \[
663	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
664	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
665	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
666	    \qquad
667	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
668	      \;\longmapsto\;
669	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
670	  \]
671	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
672	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
673	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
674	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
675	  universal family.
676	\end{theorem}
677	
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
680	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
681	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
682	    def:grassmannian_functor}
683	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
684	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
685	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
686	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
687	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
688	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
689	
690	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
691	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
692	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
693	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
694	  the composite
695	  \[
696	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
697	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
698	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
699	  \]
700	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
701	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
702	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
703	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
704	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
705	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
706	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
707	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
708	
709	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
710	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
711	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
712	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
713	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
714	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
715	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
716	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
717	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
718	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
719	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
720	
721	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
722	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
723	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
724	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
725	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
726	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
727	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
728	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
729	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
730	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
731	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
732	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
733	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
734	
735	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
736	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
737	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
738	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
739	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
740	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
741	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
742	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
743	
744	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
745	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
746	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
747	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
748	\end{proof}
