11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].

1840	\begin{lemma}[Mathlib anchor — transitivity of flatness]
1841	  \label{lem:mathlib_flat_trans}
1842	  \lean{Module.Flat.trans}
1843	  \mathlibok
1844	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Stability}).}
1845	  Let \(R \to S\) be a ring homomorphism and \(M\) an \(S\)-module, viewed as an
1846	  \(R\)-module through the scalar tower. If \(S\) is flat over \(R\) and \(M\) is
1847	  flat over \(S\), then \(M\) is flat over \(R\).
1848	\end{lemma}
1849	
1850	\begin{lemma}[Mathlib anchor — flatness over a base is local on a spanning family of the source]
1851	  \label{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}
1852	  \lean{Module.flat_of_isLocalized_span}
1853	  \mathlibok
1854	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Localization}).}
1855	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S\) an \(R\)-algebra, and \(M\) an \(S\)-module,
1856	  regarded as an \(R\)-module through the scalar tower \(R \to S \to M\). Let
1857	  \(s \subseteq S\) be a subset whose ideal span is all of \(S\)
1858	  (\(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,s = \top\)), and for each \(r \in s\) let \(M_r\) be an
1859	  away-localization of \(M\) at \(r\) (an \(S_r\)-module, hence an \(R\)-module via the
1860	  tower). If \(M_r\) is flat over \(R\) for every \(r \in s\), then \(M\) is flat over
1861	  \(R\). \emph{(Flatness over the base ring \(R\) descends from a spanning family of
1862	  principal localizations of the \emph{source} ring \(S\).)}
1863	\end{lemma}
1864	
1865	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1866	[G3.1 — free patch implies flat patch]
1867	  \label{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat}
1868	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_patch_free_imp_flat}
1869	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}
1870	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, for every \(j\) the
1871	  localized section module \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1872	\end{lemma}
1873	
1874	\begin{proof}
1875	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}
1876	  By hypothesis \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and a free module over a
1877	  commutative ring is flat (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}). Hence \((M_j)_f\) is
1878	  flat over \(A_f\).
1879	\end{proof}
1880	
1881	\begin{lemma}[G3.2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\)]
1882	  \label{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base}
1883	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_stalk_flat_over_base}
1884	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves,
1885	        lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1886	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, for every point
1887	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is flat over the local ring
1888	  \(\OO_{S,\,p(x)}\).
1889	\end{lemma}
1890	
1891	\begin{proof}
1892	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves,
1893	        lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1894	  Fix \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). Choose a patch
1895	  \(W_j \ni x\) from the cover. On \(W_j\) quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\)
1896	  with \(\widetilde{M_j}\), where \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) is a finite \(B_j\)-module
1897	  (\cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); by
1898	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} the sections over a basic open
1899	  \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\), and passing to the limit
1900	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to
1901	  \(x\). Since \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) acts invertibly on the local ring
1902	  \(\OO_{S,s}\) and hence on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the
1903	  inverted module \((M_j)_f\), and \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the
1904	  prime of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)). By
1905	  \cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat} the module \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), and
1906	  localization preserves flatness (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves}), so
1907	  \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s} = \OO_{S,\,p(x)}\).
1908	\end{proof}
1909	
1910	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1911	[G3.3 — flatness over a local base is local on the source affine]
1912	  \label{lem:gf_flat_base_local_on_source}
1913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_base_local_on_source}
1914	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal}
1915	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(B\) an \(R\)-algebra, and \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1916	  regarded as an \(R\)-module through the scalar tower \(R \to B \to N\). Suppose
1917	  that for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak q \subset B\) the localization
1918	  \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) is flat over \(R\). Then \(N\) is flat over \(R\).
1919	\end{lemma}
1920	
1921	\begin{proof}
1922	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal}
1923	  This is the source-locality criterion for flatness over a fixed base ring,
1924	  instantiated with the canonical localizations \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) of \(N\) at the
1925	  maximal ideals \(\mathfrak q\) of \(B\): each \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) is a localized
1926	  module of \(N\) at the prime \(\mathfrak q\) of the source ring \(B\), and the
1927	  hypothesis supplies the required flatness of every \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) over \(R\).
1928	  \Cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal} then yields that \(N\) is flat over
1929	  \(R\). \emph{(Note: unlike \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal}, which
1930	  localizes over the same ring it tests flatness over, here \(N\) is localized at the
1931	  primes of the source ring \(B\) while flatness is tested over the distinct base
1932	  ring \(R\).)}
1933	\end{proof}
1934	
1935	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1936	[G3.4 — stalk flatness over the local base via transitivity]
1937	  \label{lem:gf_stalk_flat_localBase}
1938	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_stalk_flat_localBase}
1939	  \uses{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base, lem:mathlib_localization_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_trans}
1940	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, let \(x \in V = D(f)\) and
1941	  let \(y \in p^{-1}(V)\) be a source point with \(p(y)\) a generization of \(x\).
1942	  Then the stalk \(\F_y\) is flat over the local ring \(\OO_{S,x}\).
1943	\end{lemma}
1944	
1945	\begin{proof}
1946	  \uses{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base, lem:mathlib_localization_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_trans}
1947	  By \cref{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base}, \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\). As
1948	  \(p(y)\) is a generization of \(x\), the comparison map
1949	  \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,\,p(y)}\) is a localization, hence \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\) is flat
1950	  over \(\OO_{S,x}\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_localization_flat}). Transitivity of flatness
1951	  along \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,\,p(y)} \to \F_y\)
1952	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_trans}) upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\)'' to
1953	  ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''.
1954	\end{proof}
1955	
1956	\begin{lemma}[B1 — localizing the source ring preserves flatness over the base]
1957	  \label{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1958	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1959	  Let \(R \to B \to N\) be a scalar tower in which \(N\) is a \(B\)-module, and let
1960	  \(T \subseteq B\) be a submonoid of the \emph{source} ring \(B\). View \(N\) as an
1961	  \(R\)-module through the tower. If \(N\) is flat over \(R\), then the localized module
1962	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\) is again flat over \(R\). \emph{(Note: the existing
1963	  Mathlib results \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves} localize over a
1964	  submonoid of the \emph{base} \(R\); here the submonoid lives in the source \(B\) while
1965	  the base \(R\) is untouched, so a separate argument is required.)}
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	
1968	\begin{proof}
1969	  Localization at \(T\) commutes with tensoring against an \(R\)-module: for every
1970	  \(R\)-module \(K\) there is a natural isomorphism
1971	  \[
1972	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1973	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K,
1974	  \]
1975	  natural in \(K\) (compatible with the maps induced by any \(R\)-linear \(K \to K'\)).
1976	  Flatness of \(N\) over \(R\) means that for every injection \(K \hookrightarrow K'\) of
1977	  \(R\)-modules the induced map \(N \otimes_R K \to N \otimes_R K'\) is injective.
1978	  Localization at the submonoid \(T\) is an exact functor, so it preserves this
1979	  injectivity; transporting along the natural isomorphism turns the result into
1980	  injectivity of
1981	  \((\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K \to (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K'\).
1982	  As this holds for every injection of \(R\)-modules, \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\)
1983	  is flat over \(R\).
1984	\end{proof}
1985	
1986	\begin{lemma}[B2 — chart-independent section-localization on a basic open]
1987	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
1988	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
1989	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib,
1990	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1991	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, fix an affine open
1992	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) of the cover, and an element
1993	  \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\). Then:
1994	  \begin{enumerate}
1995	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits
1996	      \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the away-localization
1997	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\) of the source module
1998	      \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\); and
1999	    \item independently of the chart, \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) is the
2000	      localization of the patch module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) at the image
2001	      \(\bar g \in B_j\) of \(g\).
2002	  \end{enumerate}
2003	  Moreover the base restriction \(\Gamma(S, U) \to A_f\) identifies \(\Gamma(S, U)\) and
2004	  \(A_f\) as localizations of \(A\) (both inverting the relevant denominators of the

2095	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2096	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2097	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2098	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2099	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2100	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2101	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2102	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2104	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2105	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2106	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2107	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2108	
2109	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2110	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2111	\end{theorem}
2112	
2113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2114	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2115	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2116	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2117	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2118	% by restricting to a non-empty
2119	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2120	\begin{proof}
2121	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2122	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2123	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2124	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2125	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2126	
2127	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
2128	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
2129	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
2130	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2131	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2132	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2133	
2134	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2135	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2136	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2137	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2138	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2139	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2140	
2141	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2142	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2143	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2144	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2145	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2146	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2147	
2148	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
2149	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
2150	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to the
2151	  flatness conclusion by a \emph{source-span} descent: over any affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
2152	  one covers \(W\) by finitely many basic opens \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) aligned to the
2153	  patches, propagates \(R\)-flatness from \((M_j)_f\) to each section module
2154	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) via the source-localization lemma, and descends along the spanning
2155	  family \(\{g\}\) of \(\Gamma(X, W)\) using \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.
2156	  This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
2157	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.

13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

content:
\begin{definition}

\leanok
[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
  %  by putting
  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
  \[
    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
  \]
  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
